preFix.title: Riven oak bowl blank roughout
preFix.subtitle: Splitting and roughing a green-oak bowl blank with axe and froe
preFix.excerpt: How to rive a green-oak blank for a carved or turned bowl and rough it to shape with a carving axe. The blank is larger than a spoon blank and the riving is more demanding. Getting the blank shape right before any hollowing begins saves hours of carving time.
preFix.sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III for axe-work on bowl blanks. Newey & Drage Practical Carpentry for the froe-and-maul riving of larger sections. Museum of English Rural Life open-access archive on turned and carved domestic vessels.

content.text: A bowl
blank
roughed correctly from a
riven
billet
of
green oak

is already most of the way to a bowl. The remaining work (hollowing and final shaping) is far easier when the blank is the right thickness and profile. Oak is the traditional bowl wood in the British tradition: strong, fine-grained, durable. A log section 20 cm in diameter and 10 cm long is enough for a 18 cm bowl.
Before you start cutting.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts (blade away from body) and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool is more dangerous than a sharp one. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. Apply firm pressure, elevate, and dress. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
The wood

Oak is the durable choice for a wooden bowl. Green oak carves well under a carving axe and gouge; seasoned oak is hard and slow going. The bowl should be started green and allowed to dry in the blank stage before final hollowing begins. English oak (Quercus robur) and sessile oak (Quercus petraea) both work. Sycamore is a softer, faster-working alternative.
Riving the blank

Place the log section end-up on the chopping block. Drive the froe through the centre of the end-grain with the beetle, then lever the two halves apart. A bowl blank wants the grain running face-to-face across the bowl rather than end-to-end; this means the blank is taken from a radial split (through the pith) rather than a tangential slice.

Split one half again to give a quarter-section. The bowl blank comes from this quarter: the flat split face becomes the base of the bowl, and the curved outer face is the outside of the bowl. Trim the blank to a rough oval 22 cm long and 20 cm wide with the carving axe.
Axe roughing

Use push cuts with the carving axe to rough the outer shape. The outside of the bowl should be a gentle dome 6 cm tall at the centre. Work from the widest point toward each end; the grain runs along the bowl length, so these cuts go with the grain and lift clean chips.

Chop a shallow depression 3 cm deep in the top face to locate the bowl interior. This roughed depression guides the subsequent gouge work. The walls at this stage are 30 mm thick; they will be finished to 8 to 10 mm.
Drying the rough blank

Wrap the rough blank in a paper bag and let it dry for four to six weeks before hollowing. Drying the blank at this thickness (30 mm wall) allows any checks to form on the thick walls where they can be carved away, rather than on the thin finished walls where they would ruin the piece.
Variations
Sycamore: lighter in colour, easier to carve, also produces a food-safe bowl once oiled. The most common choice for a first food-contact bowl.
Larger bowl: scale up to a 30 cm log section. The riving and roughing take longer; the blank needs a longer drying period of six to eight weeks.

rows.fix: This is normal on a first-season blank. Remove the pith section entirely by splitting it off with the axe before the next drying cycle. The blank without the pith dries more evenly.
rows.cause: The pith-wood in the centre of the riven blank shrank faster than the outer wood
rows.symptom: Deep check appeared along the base of the blank during drying

rows.fix: Rotate the blank 180 degrees and approach the same area from the other direction. On a round blank, there are always two quadrants where the grain runs against the cut direction; switch direction for those quarters.
rows.cause: Axe cuts going against the grain on the outer dome
rows.symptom: Blank surface is rough and difficult to refine after axe work

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
